title: Matsvinskollen
subtitle: Designing a quiz-based mobile experience that turns food waste knowledge into interactive learning.

intro.heading: Can interactive learning make food waste easier to understand?
intro.body:
Matsvinskollen was a three week school project exploring how design could make food waste knowledge easier to understand and apply in everyday situations.

Instead of building a full product, we prototyped and iterated, the goal was to define a clear concept and test to further validate the idea. The result was a quiz-based mobile experience where users learn about food storage and food waste through short interactive questions.

role.heading: My role
role.body:
I worked across research synthesis, interaction design, wireframing, and prototyping.

My main contribution was defining how the experience should work and how I could define this for easier collaboration. I designed the quiz flow, created the interface, and structured the prototype in Figma using reusable components and interaction states.

problem.heading: Defining what should actually be built
problem.body:
Food waste is a broad topic, and one of the main challenges early in the project was defining a solution that could realistically be explored within three weeks.

Instead of designing a complex system, we focused on a smaller interaction concept: helping users learn practical food knowledge through quick questions and immediate feedback.

This shifted the design challenge from just presenting information to designing a clear interaction that guides users through the experience with gamification feedback in mind.
bullets: Defining a focused concept that could realistically be explored within a three-week project
Turning abstract food waste knowledge into simple, interactive learning moments
Designing a quiz interaction that feels engaging without becoming overly complex
Communicating feedback clearly so users immediately understand the impact of their choices

approach.heading: Designing the quiz interaction
approach.body:
I started by mapping the structure of the experience using wireframes to define how users move through the quiz.

From there I designed the interface and built the interactive prototype in Figma. Using Auto Layout and reusable components that helped keep the design consistent and made it easier to iterate as the concept evolved.

image.alt: Wireframe of the Matsvinskollen quiz flow

interactionSystem.heading: Designing the interaction system
interactionSystem.body:
Because the experience revolves around answering questions, clear feedback was essential.

I designed reusable UI components that communicate different interaction states, including default selection, correct answers, and incorrect answers.

Structuring these states as components made the prototype easier to maintain and ensured the interaction stayed consistent throughout the experience.

image.alt: Component-based interaction states designed in Figma

outcome.heading: A structured and testable concept
outcome.body:
The final concept was a mobile quiz experience where users answer questions and receive immediate feedback.

By defining the interaction flow and building reusable components, the prototype became easier to test and iterate during user sessions.

image.alt: Prototype flow of the Matsvinskollen quiz experience

keyLearning.heading: Structure makes ideas easier to test
keyLearning.body:
One of the biggest lessons from this project was how important it is to define the structure of a concept early.

Even a simple idea becomes much easier to test and iterate on when the interaction flow, UI states, and system behaviour are clearly designed.

cards.title: Structure before detail
cards.body: Starting with wireframes and flow mapping helped define how users move through the quiz before focusing on visual design.

cards.title: Components support iteration
cards.body: Building reusable components and interaction states in Figma made it easier to adjust layouts and update screens without breaking the overall structure.
